docs.title: Enigma Documentation Portal
docs.text:
Welcome to the official documentation for Enigma, a modern, lightweight cryptography system for secure password hashing and symmetric byte/file encryption.

Enigma is built to deliver native, high-performance cryptographic operations through a secure C++ engine paired with standard Python interfaces.

docs.title: Key Capabilities
docs.text:
• 🔑 Secure Password Hashing: Strengthens user credentials with a memory-hard iteration workflow that defends against GPU-based offline dictionary attacks.
  • 🔒 Symmetric File Cipher: Encrypts and decrypts files of any size using block-level bit rotation and XORing.
  • 📦 In-Memory Byte Encryption: Secures variables and raw byte streams directly in-memory using fast Python bindings.
  • 💻 Cross-Platform CLI: A portable command-line tool compiled for Windows, macOS, and Linux.
  • 🐍 Standard Python Extension: Native speed combined with Pythonic interfaces and exceptions.
  • 🔄 Cross-Compatibility: Decrypt files on the CLI that were encrypted using the Python package, and vice-versa.

docs.title: Project Purpose
docs.text:
Enigma was created to provide a lightweight, auditable cryptography tool that runs out of the box with zero runtime dependencies.

[!NOTE] Enigma relies on custom-designed encryption and hashing processes. It is designed for educational, utility, and obfuscation use cases, and is not recommended for high-security applications where standard AES-GCM or Argon2id systems are required. See the Security Warnings section for full details.

docs.title: Command Line Interface (CLI)
docs.text: The native command-line utility provides terminal-based operations to hash passwords, encrypt files, and decrypt files.

docs.title: 🛠️ Global Command Syntax
docs.text:
The executable (enigma or enigma.exe) is invoked as follows:

enigma <command> [arguments...]

Exposed commands: * hash: Hash a password credentials string. * encrypt: Encrypt any file. * decrypt: Decrypt an encrypted file.

docs.title: 🔑 hash
docs.text: Generate an Enigma password hash.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: enigma hash <password> [cost] [salt]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text:
• <password> (Required): The password string to hash.
  • [cost] (Optional, default 10): The work factor factor ($2^{\text{cost}}$ iterations). Must be between 0 and 31.
  • [salt] (Optional): A 16-character salt string. If omitted, a cryptographically secure random salt is generated.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Hash a password using default cost 10 and a random salt:
enigma hash "mySecurePassword"

# Hash a password with custom cost 12:
enigma hash "mySecurePassword" 12

# Hash a password using a fixed salt:
enigma hash "mySecurePassword" 10 "fixedSalt1234567"

docs.title: 🔒 encrypt
docs.text: Encrypt any file. By default, it outputs a file with an .enc extension using the custom ENIGMA01 binary format.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: enigma encrypt <file> <password> [cost] [output_file]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text:
• <file> (Required): Path to the input file to encrypt.
  • <password> (Required): The encryption password.
  • [cost] (Optional, default 10): Hashing cost factor used for deriving the encryption key.
  • [output_file] (Optional): Path to write the encrypted output. If omitted, defaults to <file>.enc.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Encrypts 'data.json' -> 'data.json.enc'
enigma encrypt data.json "SecretPassphrase"

# Encrypts 'data.json' -> 'archive.bin' with custom cost 12
enigma encrypt data.json "SecretPassphrase" 12 archive.bin

docs.title: 🔓 decrypt
docs.text: Decrypt any ENIGMA01 formatted file.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: enigma decrypt <file> <password> [output_file]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text:
• <file> (Required): Path to the encrypted file.
  • <password> (Required): Decryption password.
  • [output_file] (Optional): Path to save the decrypted output. If omitted, defaults to <file>.dec.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Decrypts 'archive.bin' -> 'archive.bin.dec'
enigma decrypt archive.bin "SecretPassphrase"

# Decrypts 'archive.bin' -> 'restored_data.json'
enigma decrypt archive.bin "SecretPassphrase" restored_data.json

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: Get up and running with Enigma as a Python library or as a standalone native command-line utility.

docs.title: 🐍 Python Library
docs.text:
The official Python package is published as enigma-encryption. It includes pre-compiled binary wheels for all major platforms (Windows, macOS, and Linux) running Python 3.10 to 3.13.

Install it directly via your package manager:

pip install enigma-encryption

Once installed, it can be imported in your Python scripts as enigma:

import enigma

docs.title: 💻 C++ Command-Line Tool (CLI)
docs.text:
For command-line use, you can download the standalone, pre-compiled binary (enigma or enigma.exe) directly.

This standalone tool requires no compiler or dependencies on your system path.

docs.title: Downloading the Executable
docs.text:
1. Go to the GitHub Releases Page.
  2. Download the binary package corresponding to your operating system:
  3. Windows: enigma-windows-amd64.zip (contains enigma.exe)
  4. macOS: enigma-macos-universal.tar.gz
  5. Linux: enigma-linux-amd64.tar.gz
  6. Extract the archive and place the executable in a directory on your system path (e.g., /usr/local/bin on Linux/macOS or a custom folder added to your Windows Path environment variables).

To verify the installation, open your terminal and run:

enigma --help

docs.title: Python Integration Guide
docs.text: The enigma package provides a high-performance Python interface to Enigma's password hashing and symmetric encryption algorithms.

docs.title: 🔑 Hashing & Verification
docs.text: Use hashing functions to stretch password credentials and verify user logins.

docs.title: Hashing API
docs.text:
def hash_password(password: str, cost: int = 10, salt: str = None) -> str:
* Returns: A formatted string: $<salt>$/$<hash>. * Raises: ValueError if cost is out of bounds or if salt length is not exactly 16.

docs.title: Salt Extraction API
docs.text:
def extract_salt(hash_str: str) -> str:
* Returns: The extracted 16-character salt string. * Raises: ValueError if the hash string format is invalid.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
import enigma

# Hash a password (automatically generates a random salt)
hashed = enigma.hash_password("MySecret123", cost=10)
print("Hash:", hashed) # e.g. $J&%Z1n_N/boCGJef$/$aBcD...

# Verify a password login:
salt = enigma.extract_salt(hashed)
is_correct = enigma.hash_password("MySecret123", cost=10, salt=salt) == hashed
assert is_correct is True

docs.title: 🔒 In-Memory Byte Encryption
docs.text: Secure byte arrays, variables, or transient memory streams directly in RAM.

docs.title: Encryption API
docs.text:
def encrypt_bytes(data: bytes, password: str, cost: int = 10) -> bytes:
* Returns: bytes containing the binary header and the ciphertext payload.

docs.title: Decryption API
docs.text:
def decrypt_bytes(data: bytes, password: str) -> bytes:
* Returns: Decrypted bytes buffer. * Raises: ValueError if validation tag checks fail (wrong password) or formatting is invalid.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
import enigma

payload = b"Top secret database connection string."

# Encrypt
encrypted = enigma.encrypt_bytes(payload, "DBPassword123")

# Decrypt
decrypted = enigma.decrypt_bytes(encrypted, "DBPassword123")
assert decrypted == payload

docs.title: 💾 File Encryption
docs.text: Encrypt and decrypt files directly on disk. Large files are processed in chunks to limit memory footprint.

docs.title: Encryption API
docs.text: def encrypt_file(input_path: str, output_path: str, password: str, cost: int = 10) -> None:

docs.title: Decryption API
docs.text:
def decrypt_file(input_path: str, output_path: str, password: str) -> None:
* Raises: ValueError if verification fails (wrong password) or IOError if files cannot be opened.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
import enigma

# Secure file
enigma.encrypt_file("config.json", "config.json.enc", "KeyPassphrase")

# Restore file
enigma.decrypt_file("config.json.enc", "config_restored.json", "KeyPassphrase")

docs.title: Security Considerations & Warnings
docs.text:
The cryptography algorithms in Enigma are custom-designed and written without external, peer-reviewed industrial libraries like OpenSSL, Libsodium, or BoringSSL.

Before using Enigma, it is critical to understand its security properties and target use cases.

docs.title: ⚡ Strengths
docs.text:
• Iterative Work Factor: By utilizing a work factor (cost), key derivation requires $2^{\text{cost}}$ iterations. This creates computational delays, defending against quick automated dictionary/brute-force attacks.
  • Memory-Hard Hashing: Intermediate rounds reference a stateful lookup matrix (memo). This forces the hashing runner to utilize RAM, reducing the efficiency of custom FPGA/GPU parallel cracking setups.
  • No Upstream Dependencies: A zero-dependency model reduces supply chain threat vectors and eliminates binary loading or dynamic linking issues.

docs.title: 1. Custom Cryptographic Algorithms
docs.text:
• No Academic Peer Review: Unlike standard algorithms (such as AES or ChaCha20), Enigma's bitwise whole-buffer rotation and XOR cipher has not undergone extensive academic cryptanalysis or peer evaluation.
  • Potential Flaws: Custom encryption schemes are highly susceptible to mathematical weaknesses, shortcut attacks, or linear/differential cryptanalysis that could compromise keys or plaintext.

docs.title: 2. Lack of Authenticated Encryption (AEAD)
docs.text:
• No Integrity Signature: Enigma does not support Authenticated Encryption (AEAD) or message authentication codes (MACs).
  • Ciphertext Tampering: If an attacker intercepts your encrypted file and alters bits within the ciphertext, Enigma's decryption engine will not raise a signature validation error. It will proceed to decrypt the altered bytes anyway, producing corrupted/garbage data in the modified segments. Only password verification tags at the header level are validated.

docs.title: 🎓 Recommended Guidelines
docs.text:
[!WARNING] Do not use Enigma to secure high-value commercial systems, financial data, health records, or authentication backends.

For standard production systems requiring certified security, you should use industry-standard ciphers: * Symmetric Encryption: Use AES-GCM-256 or ChaCha20-Poly1305. * Password Hashing: Use Argon2id or scrypt. * Library Options: Standard production libraries include PyNaCl (Libsodium) or Python's native cryptography package.

docs.title: Enigma Developer Portal
docs.text: Welcome to the Enigma Developer Portal. This section contains architecture details, algorithm mathematics, build instructions, and testing frameworks designed for contributors and auditors.

docs.title: 🛠️ Development Philosophy
docs.text:
• Single Source of Truth: All cryptographic calculations (hashing, salting, encryption, decryption) live strictly inside the C++ core library target. CLI and Python bindings link this code statically.
  • Zero External Dependencies: The repository must not depend on external dynamic binaries (such as OpenSSL) to ensure portability.
  • Strict Cross-Check Verification: Tests must verify that CLI commands and Python binding returns match outputs identically on all inputs.

docs.title: 📖 Developer Guides
docs.text:
Explore the following sections to understand or contribute to the project:

  1. Architecture Walkthrough: Diagrams the component interactions between C++, pybind11, pytest, and deployment pipelines. Includes a map of where source code files reside.
  2. Cryptographic Algorithms: Deep dive into the mathematical formulas of the sub-hashes, index permutations, memo lookup tables, and bitwise whole-buffer block rotations.
  3. Compiling & Testing Setup: Detailed steps to set up local compilers (MSVC, GCC, Clang), configure CMake, compile binaries, and execute testing suites (ctest and pytest).

docs.title: Cryptographic Algorithms
docs.text: This section details the custom cryptographic processes implemented inside Enigma's core engine.

docs.title: 🔑 Hashing Internals
docs.text: The hashing routine internal_hash_password mixes inputs into printable ASCII sequences using modular math and polynomial equations.

docs.title: 1. hash1 (Character Mixing)
docs.text:
Processes the initial byte sequence $S = \text{salt} + \text{"$"} + \text{password}$:

$$\text{accumulator} = \text{seed}$$

For each byte $b_j$ in the sequence:

$$\text{val} = \text{accumulator} \times 113 + b_j$$

$$\text{accumulator} = \text{accumulator} + 2 \pmod{23}$$

$$\text{char_code} = (\text{val} \pmod{90}) \ \& \ \left( \left\lfloor \frac{\text{val}}{90} \right\rfloor \pmod{90} + 37 \right)$$

The resulting char_code byte is emitted to the output buffer hash1.

docs.title: 2. Permutation Step
docs.text:
Removes elements from hash1 one-by-one at cyclic offsets:

$$\text{index} = (\text{current} + n - 1) \pmod{\text{size}}$$

This produces a specific permutation of the character array rather than a standard sort.

docs.title: 3. hash2 (Polynomial Hash, Base 71)
docs.text:
Computes a base-71 polynomial value:

$$hv_{next} = hv \times 71 + b_k$$

For each generated state, we emit printable ASCII codes by extracting digits: $$\text{code} = (hv \pmod{90}) + 37$$

$$hv = \left\lfloor \frac{hv}{90} \right\rfloor$$

docs.title: 4. hash3 (Polynomial Hash, Base 997)
docs.text:
Computes a base-997 polynomial hash.

[!IMPORTANT] To preserve state dependency, the final value of the accumulator $hv$ from hash2 is not reset; it is carried directly into hash3 as the starting accumulator seed.

docs.title: 🔒 Cipher Internals (Rotate + XOR)
docs.text: The cipher divides files and buffers into 1024-byte chunks (blocks).

docs.title: 1. Rounds Calculation
docs.text:
For each block at index $i$:

$$\text{Rounds} = 10 + (i \pmod 6)$$

This ensures that consecutive blocks are processed with varying iteration depths ($10 \le \text{Rounds} \le 15$), breaking static pattern analysis.

docs.title: 2. Rotation and XOR
docs.text:
In each round $n$:

  1. Shift Calculation: The block is rotated left bitwise by: $$\text{shift} = 1 + ((\text{key_byte}[n] + n) \pmod 7) \text{ bits}$$
  2. Bitwise Left Rotation: Bits are shifted left across the entire 1 KB block. The overflow bits of byte $j$ are carried over into byte $j-1$, and the leftmost bits of byte 0 are wrapped around to the rightmost positions of byte 1023.
  3. XOR: Each byte of the rotated block is XORed with the corresponding derived key byte.

docs.title: Monorepo Architecture
docs.text: Enigma is configured as a C++ monorepo built using a unified CMake configuration, bundled with python bindings compiled using scikit-build-core.

docs.title: 🗺️ Codebase Map
docs.text:
All cryptographic algorithms reside in the core library and are statically linked by the frontends.

enigma/
├── CMakeLists.txt              # Unified root CMake config
├── pyproject.toml              # scikit-build-core python extension packaging
│
├── core/                       # ✅ SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH (C++ Static Library)
│   ├── CMakeLists.txt          # Defines 'enigma_core' library target
│   ├── include/enigma/
│   │   ├── hash.h              # Hashing declarations
│   │   ├── cipher.h            # Cipher declarations
│   │   └── primitives.h        # Low-level bitwise declarations
│   └── src/
│       ├── hash.cpp            # Custom password hashing logic
│       ├── salt.cpp            # CSPRNG generator (Windows Advapi32/Linux /dev/urandom)
│       ├── primitives.cpp      # Whole-buffer bitwise shifts & rotations
│       ├── cipher.cpp          # Symmetric encryption block-level wrapper
│       └── file_io.cpp         # Chunked C++ stream file reader/writer
│
├── cli/                        # Native C++ CLI executable
│   ├── CMakeLists.txt          # Defines 'enigma' executable target
│   └── src/main.cpp            # CLI command parser and Unicode Windows converter
│
├── python/                     # pybind11 Python Native Module
│   ├── CMakeLists.txt          # Configures pybind11 FetchContent
│   ├── bindings.cpp            # C++ pybind11 exports & error translations
│   └── enigma/
│       ├── __init__.py         # Re-exports compiled C++ module
│       └── __init__.pyi        # PEP 561 static type signature stubs
│
└── tests/                      # Unified Test Suites
    ├── cpp/                    # GoogleTest / Standard C++ unit tests
    └── python/                 # Pytest suite & CLI/Python cross-compatibility verification

docs.title: ⚙️ Component Interaction
docs.text:
The diagram below details how C++ static modules link into the dynamic python binders and binary executables:

graph TD
    subgraph "Core Cryptography Layer"
        H[core/src/hash.cpp] -->|encapsulated| Lib[enigma_core STATIC]
        S[core/src/salt.cpp] -->|encapsulated| Lib
        P[core/src/primitives.cpp] -->|encapsulated| Lib
        C[core/src/cipher.cpp] -->|encapsulated| Lib
        F[core/src/file_io.cpp] -->|encapsulated| Lib
    end

    subgraph "Native C++ CLI"
        Main[cli/src/main.cpp] -->|statically links| Lib
    end

    subgraph "Python Native Extension"
        Bind[python/bindings.cpp] -->|statically links| Lib
        PyInit[python/enigma/__init__.py] -->|loads| Bind
    end

    subgraph "Verification Pipelines"
        CppTest[tests/cpp/] -.->|verifies| Lib
        PyTest[tests/python/] -.->|imports| PyInit
        Cross[tests/python/cross_check.py] -.->|calls| Main
        Cross -.->|calls| PyInit
    end

docs.title: 🔗 Key C++ File References
docs.text: For developers auditing or extending Enigma, these are the core files to investigate: * core/src/hash.cpp: Contains the custom password stretching algorithm with state arrays. * core/src/cipher.cpp: Owns the ENIGMA01 file header writing and password verification tag logic. * core/src/primitives.cpp: Core math functions for rotating whole 1024-byte memory buffers. * python/bindings.cpp: Binds Python parameters, validates memory structures, and converts C++ errors (std::runtime_error) into Pythonic ValueError or IOError structures.

docs.title: Compiling & Testing Guide
docs.text: This guide describes how to configure your local development environment, compile the C++ binaries, run the testing suites, and manage python packaging.

docs.title: 🛠️ Build Prerequisites
docs.text: Verify that your system has the following tools installed: * CMake (v3.18 or newer) * C++ Compiler (supporting standard C++17) * Windows: MSVC 2019+ or MinGW-w64 (GCC 8+) * macOS: Apple Clang (Xcode 10.3+) * Linux: GCC 8+ or Clang 7+ * Python (v3.8 or newer) with pip and virtualenv

docs.title: 💻 Local Compilation (C++)
docs.text:
To configure, compile, and run the C++ targets:

# 1. Configure CMake build tree
cmake -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release

# 2. Build CLI and tests in parallel
cmake --build build --parallel

The resulting binaries will be created at: * CLI Executable: build/cli/enigma (or enigma.exe) * Test Executable: build/tests/cpp/hash_tests and cipher_tests

docs.title: 🧪 Testing Suites
docs.text: Enigma has parallel C++ and Python verification paths.

docs.title: 1. C++ CTest Suite
docs.text:
Runs standard regression tests for the core hashing arrays and block rotation states.

cd build
ctest --output-on-failure

docs.title: 2. Python Pytest Suite
docs.text:
Runs verification checks, parameter error boundaries, and algorithm parity checks.

# Install the package in editable mode
pip install -e . --no-build-isolation

# Run the pytest suite
python -m pytest tests/python/ -v

docs.title: 📦 Python Packaging & Cibuildwheel
docs.text:
Python packaging is managed by scikit-build-core as defined in pyproject.toml.

When publishing a tag v*.*.* to GitHub: 1. Cibuildwheel executes across a matrix of ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, and windows-latest runners. 2. It builds wheels targeting Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, and 3.13 (cp310-* to cp313-*). 3. On Windows, it dynamically builds via MinGW with flags to link C++ runtimes (-static-libgcc, -static-libstdc++, -Wl,-Bstatic,--whole-archive -lwinpthread) statically. This prevents runtime errors on client systems lacking GCC/MinGW runtimes. 4. Once built, wheels are automatically uploaded to PyPI using Trusted Publishing.
